chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Acceleration of rolling body on incline: a = g sinθ / (1 + k²/R²)
Solid sphere (k²/R² = 2/5): a = 5g sinθ/7 — fastest roller
Ring/hollow cylinder (k²/R² = 1): a = g sinθ/2 — slowest roller
Acceleration is independent of mass and radius — depends only on shape (k²/R²)
Minimum friction for rolling without slipping: f = mg sinθ × (k²/R²)/(1 + k²/R²)
The body with smallest k²/R² reaches the bottom first
truth_statements: A solid sphere rolls down an incline faster than a hollow sphere, disc, or ring regardless of their masses or radii
The acceleration of a rolling body down an incline depends only on the ratio k²/R² and the incline angle, not on mass or size
Static friction is required for rolling on an incline and does no work
The velocity at the bottom of an incline (starting from rest) is v = √(2gh/(1 + k²/R²))
A sliding block (no rolling) reaches the bottom faster than any rolling body because none of the PE goes into rotational KE

aha_moment.what_to_show: A race down an incline: solid sphere, disc, hollow sphere, and ring released simultaneously. Solid sphere wins every time. A sliding block (no friction) beats them all. The k²/R² ratio determines the ranking — NOT mass or size.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Four objects racing with position markers and k²/R² values displayed — solid sphere always leads
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect heavier or larger objects to roll faster — seeing mass and radius cancel out while shape determines speed is a powerful insight

simulation_states.label: Single body rolling
simulation_states.description: A solid sphere rolling down an incline. Forces shown: mg sinθ (along incline), friction f (up the incline), normal N. The body accelerates at a = g sinθ/(1 + k²/R²).
simulation_states.emphasis: Friction enables rolling but reduces net acceleration compared to sliding

simulation_states.label: Energy split
simulation_states.description: PE bar converts to translational KE + rotational KE. For a sphere: 5/7 goes to translation, 2/7 to rotation. For ring: 1/2 each. More rotation = less translation = slower.
simulation_states.emphasis: More KE going to rotation means less speed — that's why rings are slowest

simulation_states.label: The rolling race
simulation_states.description: Four shapes released simultaneously: solid sphere (fastest), disc, hollow sphere, ring (slowest). A sliding block beats all. Rankings shown with k²/R² values.
simulation_states.emphasis: Shape determines speed — mass and radius don't matter! Sliding beats rolling.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student selects which shapes to race, adjusts incline angle. Timer shows which reaches bottom first. Toggle friction off for one body to see it slide (faster but no rolling).
simulation_states.emphasis: Change the angle, pick different shapes — the ranking never changes!

common_misconceptions.correction: Mass cancels out in the rolling acceleration formula: a = g sinθ/(1 + k²/R²). Only the shape factor k²/R² matters. A tiny solid sphere and a huge solid sphere reach the bottom at the same time.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Race two spheres of different masses — they arrive together

common_misconceptions.belief: A rolling body and a sliding body reach the bottom at the same time
common_misconceptions.correction: A sliding body (no friction, no rolling) reaches the bottom FASTER because all PE converts to translational KE. In rolling, some PE goes to rotational KE, leaving less for translation.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Race a rolling sphere against a sliding block — block always wins
